text: A schematic view shows the port placement for reduced port robotic rectal surgery, including a 3.5 cm vertical incision through the umbilical skin with a glove port inserted into the intra-abdominal space. An additional single port for wristed robotic instruments and rectal retraction is inserted in the right lower quadrant. The operative view displays the access port setup for the procedure.

The surgeon performs a totally robotic approach using dual docking technique, dissecting lymph nodes around the root of the inferior mesenteric artery. High ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery is achieved using a hemlock clip plier through the artery arm port.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection, identifying the gonadal vessels and ureter. The dissection progresses along the left pericolic gutter, reaching up to the level of the spleen.

The robotic cart is repositioned between the patient's legs for the pelvic stage. The assistant surgeon uses the additional single port to retract the recto-sigmoid cephalad, facilitating access to the pelvic structures.

The surgeon replaces two 250 mm curved cannulas with two 300 mm curved cannulas to facilitate deep pelvic dissection. The dissection follows the principles of total mesorectal excision and autonomic nerve preservation, with posterior pelvic dissection along the rectal proper fascia, enveloping the rectum and mesorectum while preserving the inferior hypogastric nerves.

The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral aspect of the mesorectal fascia from the pelvic plexus, identifying dense adhesions between these structures. These attachments are meticulously separated, revealing the left middle rectal artery, which is controlled using monopolar diathermy.

The surgeon performs anterolateral dissection, progressing until reaching the previously dissected area at the lateral wall. The dissection continues posterior to the vagina, with the magnified robotic view aiding in identifying the correct surgical plane.
The surgeon performs distal bowel resection using three 45mm endo-wrist staplers inserted through the additional single port located in the right lower quadrant.

The surgeon extracts the tumor specimen through the transumbilical incision. An end-to-end intracorporeal anastomosis is performed using a double stapling technique. A suction drain is placed in the pelvic cavity through the additional single port site.
